sentences.text: Well, Anthony Eden was a hunchbacked colonel who was very, very kind.
Whenever we had any problems or needed anything, we could-- or his door was always open.
We could go to him.
We really didn't need much because we had our own rooms, maybe two or three to a room, two or three refugees.
And I remember I'd shared with somebody called Helmut Riehls and Harry Willing, or Harold Swilling, as he was called originally.
And we really had a wonderful life because most of the time we did-- simply, we kept our hotel clean, because we were stationed in Isle of Man hotels.
And the rest of the time, we could either go swimming, or we could play tennis.
So we had the full facilities of a luxury vacation resort without paying a penny for it.
Afterwards-- we stayed for about two or three months, as I said.
That was in 1940.
We were interned in 1940, right after the fall of France.
And when I was finally released, I could go-- first, I went to a youth hostel.
And in this youth hostel in Evesham, we were treated extremely well.
We had a wonderful time.
And finally, also, I could-- later on, at least-- go and spend some time with my sponsor, Sir Eustace Pulbrook, the chairman of Lloyd's of London.
And so I really did not have to suffer very much in England during the war.
But later on, after my youth hostel experiences, when I went to London, first, I stayed in a place called King Henry's Road.
And then, later, in Camden Town in-- on Camden Road.
No, these were youth hostels specially for German and Austrian refugees like me, youngsters like me.
In London, yes.
Oddly enough, we could go wherever we wanted to in London itself.
And of course, when we left London for any place-- which happened for any reason, which I hardly ever did-- we did not need-- we did need a special passport, local passports at least.
But I hardly ever left London.
Yes.
What I actually did was, at first, I worked for a munitions factory.
We made boxes in which the bombs were kept before they were loaded onto the planes to bomb Nazi Germany.
That was my first job.
But I didn't enjoy it too much because it was pretty boring.
And I just-- especially in winter, it was always-- it was never heated, the factory.
It was bitterly cold, a bitterly cold winter.
And finally, I went to a place.
And they sold clothing and cloth itself, bales of cloth.
It was a Greek manufacturer just off Oxford Street.
And I stayed with him for quite some time and had, really, a pretty good time there.
Well--
No, I had to support myself.
Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we made our money-- we were given a salary.
And from this salary, we had to pay for our board and lodging and food.
And that was that.
Personally, as far as I remember, I never had to ask anybody for a penny.
But the Bloomsbury House, which was in charge of the refugees, we always had-- we checked them maybe once every four or six weeks.
And my case worker, Mr.
Reuben, was very, very kind.
And he was actually the person who later informed me about the death of my family and the survival of my sister.
Yeah, the Bloomsbury House actually used to be called the Bloomsbury Hotel, which is somewhere in-- off-- near Grosvenor Square-- or I think it's-- it's near Russell Street.
It used to be a hotel.
And it was converted into a office building only for Jewish and non-Jewish German and Austrian refugees.
And as I said, my case worker was Mr.
Reuben, who had an office there.
And whenever we had any problems, we went to him, and he tried to solve it for us.
And once, I stayed with-- as I said, with two other boys in my hostel in one room.
We fidgeted with the gas burner.
We didn't have enough money.
So we somehow fidgeted with it and adjusted it to such an extent that we could pass as much gas as we wanted to without having to pay a penny for it.
But when the gas bill arrived, the landlord-- or landlady, Mrs.
Dudley, she noticed that our room spent something like about 30 pounds a-- a month, while all the other rooms spent only about 3 pounds.
And there were not 30 pounds in the coin box.
So she arrived with somebody one day from Scotland Yard.
And they questioned us about it.
And they questioned us all separately, the-- the three boys.
And I realized after they cornered me-- first, we lied about it, of course, and said that we never had fidgeted around with it.
But finally, I broke down and started to cry and confessed--
[LAUGHS]
But they were very nice about it.
And that was during the height of the war on.
They knew we were Germans and Austrians.
And they said, please don't do it again.
I mean, that's ridiculous.
I mean, you have enough money.
And that was the end of it.
And that was really so decent of them that we gave a large bouquet of flowers to Mrs.
Dudley and apologized.
And she started crying.
And we lived happily ever after, so to speak.
No.
That was the Bloomsbury House.
The Bloomsbury House consisted of maybe 10 or 15 different organizations looking after various kind of segments of the German and Austrian immigrants.
Some of them were Jewish.
Some of them were not Jewish.
Some of them were sick and had to be sent to sanitariums and so on.
So this Bloomsbury House and the various caseworkers, like my case worker, Mr.
Reuben, always looked after us.
We always had to report to them.
Now that applied to those and was pretty easy for those who lived in London.
But of course, they were all over the place-- and actually-- these refugees, in Lancashire, in the Midlands, in Wales, in Scotland.
Then, sometimes, these caseworkers were specially sent up to these refugees and had to discuss their problems with them and resolve them.
But in my case, I was really fortunate.
But of course, I lived in London.
And when I-- while I was still living in Worcester-- or outside Worcester, and Wales, I did not really have any problems.
Well, in the second one, in the--
--textile, I stayed for about a year with the Greek owner, and right off Oxford Circus and Oxford Street.
And he was a very, very kind guy.
But he was already around 80 years old when I started work for him.
And after a year, he said he was going to a close up and he just couldn't do it anymore.
By that time it was already 1943 or so.
Earlier, I had actually worked in Wiltshire in-- on the farm for-- in a garden and a farm for about a year-- a family called the Griffith family.
He was Welsh.
And she was French.
And they both disliked me intensely, probably because the French has-- have always disliked the Germans, regardless of conditions.
The fact that I was Jewish made no-- had no impact on her whatsoever.
And of course, at that time, I didn't know that my family had been wiped out or that they were even in concentration camps, because I learned all this later.
But I did not have a very happy experience there.
Rachel was their daughter who was constantly singing at the piano.
And she was a very heavyset young girl who never seemed to do any work.
And that annoyed me no end, too.
But after a year, I left that place and went to London.
Well, in London I worked in a factory and--
Yes.
Textile--
Then I actually went to the Bloomsbury House.
And Mr.
Reuben, he said, you know, we lost one of our-- I don't know how.
But we lost one of our social workers.
Would you like to be trained as a social worker looking after refugees like yourself?
And I said, I'd love to.
And he said, all right, we will train you.
You will have your own little office.
And you'll have to discuss the problems with these various boys and girls and see to it that everything is done to their liking and that-- that you can solve their problems, et cetera, et cetera.
And I was trained and had my own little office.
And I stayed there until I left in December-- or November 1947.
Yeah, I started looking for my family almost immediately.
And of course, the best course was, first of all, to write to the Red Cross, who couldn't help me at all, and then the Bloomsbury House itself.
And one day-- they had, apparently, contacts in Germany.
And it took quite some time.
The war ended in '45.
And I remember D-day, of course, celebrating-- V-Day, rather-- V-E Day, Victory in Europe Day, celebrating outside Buckingham Palace and singing and dancing with everybody.
But one day Mr.
Reuben, in Bloomsbury House, called me into his own personal office.
And that was in 1946 already.
He said, Paul, please sit down.
I've got some good news and some bad news for you.
Which ones would you like to hear first?
And I said, oh, I'd like to hear the bad news first and get them out of my way.
I had no way of knowing what he was going to say.
He said, it's about your family.
We just heard from the International Red Cross that both your parents were killed.
Your mother apparently died in Auschwitz and your father in Theresienstadt.

And I burst into tears, of course, because I was always secretly hoping that something would-- that they would reappear, that they may have been lost somewhere, maybe in Poland or Czechoslovakia, or wherever.
And that one day they will just appear.
And I just burst into tears because I loved my mother more than anybody else ever in my life.
And then he said, now the good news is that your sister, Anne Marie, is alive.
And of course, I burst into tears again, but this time with joy.
But the mixture-- within 60 seconds to hear this news-- was overwhelming as far as I was concerned.
But after a few minutes, you-- I got-- I wouldn't say I got used to it.
But I quieted-- calmed down.
And they said, OK, now what would you like to do?
And I said, I would like to get my-- to live with my sister again and hear are all her news.
And he said, well, it will be very hard for her to get to here-- to get her here to England.
You know that hardly anybody can emigrate right now.
But I went home and didn't quite know what to do.
And then I realized that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mrs.
Roosevelt happened to be in London at the time.
She stayed at the Dorchester Hotel, I think it was, in order to discuss the agenda for the forthcoming United Nations.
And they were planning it at least.
And so I wrote a letter to her explaining what happened to my sister-- if she could help me get my sister out of Germany.
And I really didn't expect an answer.
But within about three or four days, I had a long letter for-- from her.
I still have it here.
And she said that she feels with me very much and that I should see a certain Mrs.
Robertson or Robinson.
I can't remember.
But it's in the letter.
That I should make an appointment with her, that maybe she can help get my sister out of Germany.
So I made this appointment with her and showed her Mrs.
Roosevelt's letter.
And after I showed her this letter, everything changed.
She-- right away, she was on the phone.
And all this happened around March, I think, of 1946.
And by November or December of the same year, she was already in the United States and then helped me to come here a year later.
How did your sister survive in Germany?
My sister survived by living underground.
She was hidden by a woman called Mrs.
Klimmeck, Frau Klimmeck.
And-- who was Aryan, but was viciously, violently anti-Nazi, hated Hitler as much as any Jew could possibly hate him.
And there were quite a few Germans like that-- not enough, unfortunately.
But she was hiding her, first of all, in her house and in different buildings because she was always afraid that maybe the Gestapo might hear about it.
And so-- but she really helped to save my sister.
And she had a son by the name of [?
Johan, ?] or Joachim.
And later, after my sister came to this country, she helped Joachim, or [?
Johan, ?] to come to this country, too.
They fell in love later and married.
No, nothing.
No.
It was just done--
Just what-- because they knew they had no money.
Remember, their bank certificates, their savings accounts, everything was confiscated by the Nazis.
They-- the Jews had absolutely nothing.
Part of it already took place in 1938-- part of it, but not all of it.
I do not remember by how much, what the percentage was, whether it was 10% or 50%.
But then, later, in 1942, when the deportations to concentration camps started, then of course the entire monies saved by Jews were confiscated.
And also, by the way, of non-Jews who were sent to concentration camps.
Never.
I never met anybody, either as far as my mother in Auschwitz or my father in Theresien was concerned-- Theresienstadt was concerned.
I only have this information from the International Tracing Service in Arolsen in Germany.
And I got it in writing exactly when they were deported, with which--
What they-- Gestapo, they call it Geheime Staatspolizei transport, or Judentransport.
The date was actually listed.
And in my father's case, I think even-- in my grandmother's too, in Hamburg-- my mother's mother, Marie Fraenkel.
The date, actually-- the date of death when they were either killed or when they died in concentration camps.
I'm still in England.
My sister knew-- no, actually, it wasn't my sister.
My cousin, who still lives in Hamburg, knew a German actress in this country.
Her name's Uta Hagen.
And she's quite a well-known stage actress.
She's played in very few movies, but mostly stage.
And she's also a teacher at the Bergdorf-Hagen School of Acting.
And Ilse Fraenkel, in Hamburg, introduced-- sent a letter of introduction to Uta Hagen and said, please see Anne Marie Kuttner in New York at this and this address.
And maybe she can help you find a sponsor who will help me get from England to the United States.
And my sister saw her.
They became friends.
And Uta Hagen helped me with the affidavit.
And I arrived about a year later, already, from England.
The-- the date of my arrival in the States was December the 8th, 1947.
Well, at first, the first week or two, I think until Christmas or so, I stayed with my sister.
And then, because I knew somebody in England by the name of Lyersh Biero-- he was well-known scriptwriter for Sir Alexander Korda.
And he was also a friend of Leslie Howard's, who-- with whom I became friendly.
He introduced me, per letter, letter of introduction, to Ernst Lubitsch, who was a famous German movie director.
And he said, take this letter to Ernst and-- and also another letter to Fritz Lang.
Take this letter to those two, and I'm sure they will be able to help you.
When I arrived-- about three weeks after my arrival in the United States-- when I arrived in Hollywood.
The day I arrived, Ernst Lubitsch died.
And that was quite a blow to me, as you can imagine.
Then I made the appointment with Fritz Lang.
And he saw me about three or four weeks later and said, after he read this letter, yes, that's very, very interesting.
But I cannot help you because the entire movie studios are at a standstill, which was true.
Because at that time, Europe did not have enough dollars to pay for movies.
They needed dollars to pay for the import of food.
So the-- virtually the entire movie studios were at a standstill.
Yes?
Then I made appointments with two other heads of studios, Harry Cohn.
And Louis B.
Mayer.
Harry Cohn was the head of Columbia Studios.
And Louis B.
Mayer, the head of Metro-Goldwyn.
And I got to see them too.
It took me a few weeks.
And I remember when I saw Harry Cohn, who was completely bald.
Somebody was standing behind him massaging his scalp while he was eating peas with a knife.
I'll never forget that.
And that I had to wait about four hours in the waiting room.
By the time I got to see him, first of all, I had to go to the bathroom.
[LAUGHS] And the secondly, I was completely drained.
And I remember even Rita Hayworth coming out at the time from-- crying her eyes out, the tears streaming down her face because Harry Cohn had given her hell about something.
But finally, I arrived at his-- in his office, where he was eating his peas and having his scalp massaged.
And he looked at the letter once more that Lyersh Biero had written.
And said, listen, I was-- can you give me a job?
That's how he started.
And I said, no, I wish I could.
Right now, I-- I've got about $25 left.
That's all.
And he gave me $50.
He said, here's $50.
I cannot help you.
And then I went to Fritz Lang.
And he was very, very nice.
He actually invited me to-- [LAUGHS] --the-- the lunchroom there.
And I remember I-- something that has never happened before.
And probably, Fritz Lang hated me for the rest of my life.
Next to our table while we were having lunch, Fredric March was having lunch with somebody.
And I remember him having his meal.
He didn't finish his meal.
And he started smoking a cigarette.
He didn't like a cigarette.
And he extinguished it in his mashed potatoes.
And I got so mad because over in England, we were living on rationed food.
And we-- mashed potatoes was like sacred to us.
I got up and started shouting at him and telling him that this is disgusting, that people are starving in Europe, and you're extinguishing your cigarettes.
And-- [LAUGHS]
And they stared at me as if I came from another planet because nobody speaks to Fredric March that way.
He was one of the top stars in the entire world.
And then I sat down at my desk-- at my table again.
And Fritz Lang said, will you please come up to my office?
[LAUGHTER] I thought, gladly.
I had no idea what he wanted.
I thought he wanted to give me a job.
But he changed his mind.
He screamed at me.
He said, do you know what you're doing to me personally?
You are my guest, and you're shouting at one of the world's top stars.
And I don't want to see you again ever.
[LAUGHS]
And he literally kicked me out.
And that was the end of my experiences in Hollywood.
I interviewed a few stars for a magazine called I got this job before I left London and interviewed movie stars like Spencer Tracy, who gave me a-- a signed autograph, which I still have.
Oh, absolutely not.
No, Louis B.
Mayer simply said, there's just nothing.
Our studios are virtually at a standstill right now.
Well, I hoped-- I said, well, what can I do?
Maybe I'll wait a little bit longer.
And I started as a dishwasher for a catering establishment in Beverly Hills, which was lovely.
The food was tremendous.
We prepared meals-- and later on, I always had to wash the dishes.
But we prepared meals for movie star parties.
So I did-- then we went with our finished meals, per truck, to a movie star's home.
Delivered them, delivered the food.
And I met a few movie stars that way, including Groucho Marx.
Only a few weeks, maybe five or six weeks.
And I realized there was nothing for me to do in Hollywood.
Maybe I would come back later one day.
But it just was hopeless.
And I went back to New York.
In New York, first, I worked for a displaced persons organization.
And I was trained as a social worker there.
And we met displaced persons at the pier.
And remember, I was not a displaced person because I came from England.
The displaced persons came from the continent of Europe.
It's not-- not only Jews, but every kind of nationality and religion.

And we had to find jobs for them, make arrangements with various people all over the country in the factories and on the land, in offices, and arrange for these displaced persons to travel all over the United States.
It's called A Church World Service.
I stayed with them till about 1956--
--about eight years.
After that, I went to Italy.
And in Italy, I-- I had corresponded with the mother of-- of a woman.
